coredata.dc:title: Power ultrasound as a pretreatment to convective drying of mulberry (Morus alba L.) leaves: Impact on drying kinetics and selected quality properties
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Yang
Wang, Ping
Wang, Yilin
Kadam, Shekhar U.
Han, Yongbin
Wang, Jiandong
Zhou, Jianzhong
coredata.dc:description: The effect of ultrasound pretreatment prior to convective drying on drying kinetics and selected quality properties of mulberry leaves was investigated in this study. Ultrasound pretreatment was carried out at 25.2–117.6W/L for 5–15min in a continuous mode. After sonication, mulberry leaves were dried in a hot-air convective dryer at 60°C. The results revealed that ultrasound pretreatment not only affected the weight of mulberry leaves, it also enhanced the convective drying kinetics and reduced total energy consumption. The drying kinetics was modeled using a diffusion model considering external resistance and effective diffusion coefficient D e and mass transfer coefficient h m were identified. Both D e and h m during convective drying increased with the increase of acoustic energy density (AED) and ultrasound duration. However, D e and h m increased slowly at high AED levels. Furthermore, ultrasound pretreatment had a more profound influence on internal mass transfer resistance than on external mass transfer resistance during drying according to Sherwood numbers. Regarding the quality properties, the color, antioxidant activity and contents of several bioactive compounds of dried mulberry leaves pretreated by ultrasound at 63.0W/L for 10min were similar to that of mulberry leaves without any pretreatments. Overall, ultrasound pretreatment is effective to shorten the subsequent drying time of mulberry leaves without damaging the quality of final product.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound pretreatment
Mulberry leaves
Convective drying
Drying kinetics
Quality property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of phenolic content and assessment of antioxidant activity
Extraction of phenolic compounds from mulberry leaves
Effect of ultrasound pretreatment on drying kinetics of mulberry leaves
Quality assessment of dried mulberry leaves

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup of ultrasound pretreatment of mulberry leaves before drying. (1) ultrasound generator; (2) ultrasound transducer; (3) ultrasound cylinder probe; (4) thermostatic water bath; (5) impeller; (6) 100-mL glass beaker.
3D plots about effects of AED and ultrasound duration during ultrasound pretreatment on weight gain (a), solid loss (b), D e (c) and h m (d) of mulberry leaves.
Experimental and modeled drying kinetics of mulberry leaves carried out at 60°C and 2.5m/s.
Central composite design (CCD)for ultrasound pretreatment and solid loss and weigh gain of mulberry leaves after sonication.

row.entry: Soaking in distilled water alone for 30min

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The value in the bracket followed by AED value in each line indicated the amplitude level used.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Identified effective diffusion coefficient and external mass transfer coefficient during convective drying of mulberry leaves and the goodness of fit of the diffusion model.

row.entry: External mass transfer coefficient (h m, kg water/m2 s)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The value in the bracket followed by AED value in each line indicated the amplitude level used.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Selected physical and chemical properties of dried mulberry leaves with and without ultrasound pretreatment.

row.entry: Sample immersed in distilled water for 15min
Total chlorophyll (mg per gram of dried sample)
Total phenolics (mg gallic acid per gram of dried sample)
Total flavonoid (mg Rutin per gram of dried sample)
Antioxidant ability (ABTS assay, μmol of Trolox per gram of dried sample)
Antioxidant ability (FRAP assay, μmol of FeSO4 per gram of dried sample)
DNJ (mg per gram of dried sample)
GABA (mg per gram of dried sample)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data were averages of different samples±standard deviation. Values followed by different letters in each row indicated significant differences (p <0.05).

head.ce:title: Power ultrasound as a pretreatment to convective drying of mulberry (Morus alba L.) leaves: Impact on drying kinetics and selected quality properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing 210095, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UCD School of Biosystems and Food Engineering, University College Dublin, Belfield, Dublin 4, Ireland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: UCD School of Biosystems and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Agro-product Processing, Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Nanjing 210014, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effect of ultrasound pretreatment prior to convective drying on drying kinetics and selected quality properties of mulberry leaves was investigated in this study. Ultrasound pretreatment was carried out at 25.2–117.6W/L for 5–15min in a continuous mode. After sonication, mulberry leaves were dried in a hot-air convective dryer at 60°C. The results revealed that ultrasound pretreatment not only affected the weight of mulberry leaves, it also enhanced the convective drying kinetics and reduced total energy consumption. The drying kinetics was modeled using a diffusion model considering external resistance and effective diffusion coefficient D e and mass transfer coefficient h m were identified. Both D e and h m during convective drying increased with the increase of acoustic energy density (AED) and ultrasound duration. However, D e and h m increased slowly at high AED levels. Furthermore, ultrasound pretreatment had a more profound influence on internal mass transfer resistance than on external mass transfer resistance during drying according to Sherwood numbers. Regarding the quality properties, the color, antioxidant activity and contents of several bioactive compounds of dried mulberry leaves pretreated by ultrasound at 63.0W/L for 10min were similar to that of mulberry leaves without any pretreatments. Overall, ultrasound pretreatment is effective to shorten the subsequent drying time of mulberry leaves without damaging the quality of final product.

ce:def-description.ce:para: external mass transfer coefficient (kg water/m2 s)
initial mass of mulberry leaves before ultrasound pretreatment (kg)
mass of mulberry leaves after ultrasound pretreatment (kg)
mass of solids lost into distilled water after ultrasound pretreatment (kg)
root mean square error (kg water/kg DM)
moisture content calculated from the diffusion model (kg water/kg DM)
moisture content obtained experimentally (kg water/kg DM)
mass transport characteristic direction or independent variable
relative humidity of air at equilibrium (%)

ce:section.ce:para: Mulberry (Morus alba L.), belonging to the Moraceae family, is widely distributed in temperate, tropical and subtropical areas. The fruit, leaf, branch and bark of mulberry are good sources of bioactive compounds. Mulberry leaves are rich in chlorogenic acid, rutin, ferulic acid, catechin, isoquercitrin, polysaccharide, 1-deoxynojirimycin (DNJ), γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and many other bioactive compounds, which are related to various health-beneficial functions, including anti-oxidation, anti-obesity, anti-atherosclerosis, anti-inflammation and anti-diabetes [1–5]. In Chinese medicine, mulberry leaves are occasionally used for fever therapy and liver protection, while in Japan and Korea, mulberry leaves are consumed as a functional food by patients with diabetes mellitus [3]. In the last decade, the consumption of mulberry leaf tea has been increasing continuously in Asian countries, including Japan, China and Thailand [3,6].

Drying is an essential process to manufacture mulberry leaf tea. Traditionally, mulberry leaves are dehydrated by sun drying. Another common drying technology is hot-air convective drying. However, both technologies require longer duration for drying. Also, sun drying is climate-dependent, while convective drying requires a high amount of energy. Furthermore, high temperature during convective drying can be detrimental to the quality and bioactive compounds of food products [2]. Thus, convective drying has been combined with ultrasound, microwave and far-infrared radiation technologies to enhance the drying process and shorten the drying time [3,7,8]. On the other hand, researches have investigated various pretreatments, such as sonication, osmotic dehydration and mechanical dewatering, to improve the drying efficiency and reduce energy consumption [9,10].

Ultrasound has already been used as a pretreatment before convective drying of various food products, such as seaweed, apple, melon, parsley leaves, etc. [10–13]. All these published studies demonstrate that ultrasound pretreatment is effective to enhance the subsequent drying process and reduce the drying time. The basis of ultrasound as a pretreatment before drying is its mechanical effect and the accompanied ultrasonic cavitation phenomenon. Specifically, the propagation of ultrasound in liquid medium can cause a rapid series of alternative compression and expansion, as well as the formation of cavitation bubbles. Micro-streaming and local extremely high temperature and pressure are generated during the collapse of cavitation bubbles [14]. The mechanical force and the physical and chemical effects of ultrasound cavitation can remove the moisture strongly attached to food materials, deform porous food materials and create microscopic channels, thus enhancing the mass transfer during convective drying [11]. According to our best knowledge, ultrasound has not yet been used as a pretreatment before convective drying of mulberry leaves.

Furthermore, the construction of mathematical models with physical significance is helpful to understand the mass transfer mechanism during drying, as well as elucidate the effects of different pretreatments on subsequent drying kinetics. In many cases of simulation of convective drying, the diffusion model based on the Fick’s second law was employed. For example, diffusion models with and without external resistance were proven to be accurate to model the drying kinetics of orange peel, apple and strawberry during convective drying in the presence of ultrasound irradiation [7,15,16]. However, there are few studies using physical models to explore the effect of ultrasound pretreatment on the subsequent drying kinetics of food products.

Therefore, the aim of this study was to investigate the feasibility of ultrasound as a pretreatment to enhance the convective drying of mulberry leaves. The effects of different parameters of ultrasound on weight gain, solid loss, moisture effective diffusion coefficient and external mass transfer coefficient calculated from diffusion model were studied. Moreover, several quality parameters of dried mulberry leaves with and without ultrasound pretreatment were also examined.

Fresh mulberry (Morus alba L.) leaves, cultivar Husang, were harvested from the mulberry plantation belonging to Jiujiu Silk Co. Ltd. (Suqian, China) in the end of May, 2015. All these leaves were young leaves (the third to the tenth leaves from the top of tree). Mulberry leaves were washed, drained, sliced into sections of approximately 1.5×1.5cm, wrapped in plastic films and stored at 4°C before experiments. The initial moisture content of mulberry leaves was determined using AOAC method by putting the leaves in a forced circulating air-drying oven at 105°C for 24h [17]. The initial moisture content of fresh mulberry leaves was 2.85±0.08kgwater/kg DM (dry matter).

A 130W ultrasound probe system (VCX130, Sonics and Materials Inc., Newtown, USA) with a diameter of 3mm operating at 20kHz was used for ultrasound pretreatment. The schematic diagram of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 1 . Specifically, 3.0g of aforementioned mulberry leaves were placed in a 100-mL glass beaker and 50mL of distilled water was added. This beaker was then positioned in a thermostatic water bath with an impeller (DC-0506, Fandilang Tech, Nanjing, China), so as to maintain the temperature inside the bath at 20±0.5°C during sonication. The ultrasound probe was submerged to a depth of 30mm in the samples and the energy input was adjusted by setting the amplitude of probe. In this study, the amplitudes used included 30, 50 and 70%. The actual power dissipated in the beaker for each amplitude level was determined using the calorimetric method [18]. For this purpose, the temperature increase in 50-mL distilled water being sonicated under adiabatic conditions was recorded using a thermocouple. From the temperature versus time data, the initial temperature dT/dt was obtained through linear regression. Ultrasound power was then calculated using the following equation:
(1)
P = mCp
dT
dt
The acoustic energy density (AED) was then calculated by dividing measured power with the volume of distilled water inside. AED values for amplitude levels of 30%, 50% and 70% were 25.2, 63.0 and 117.6W/L, respectively. Ultrasound treatments were performed under a continuous mode.

After sonication, excess water from the surface of mulberry leaves was carefully removed using filter paper and the weight of sample was measured. Meanwhile, the distilled water used during ultrasound treatment was dried at 105°C until the dried samples reached constant weight. Weight gain (increase in mulberry leaf weight) and solid loss (loss of soluble solids) were then calculated as follows:
(2)
Weight gain
( % ) =
m
us
-
m
0
m
0
× 100
(3)
Solid loss
( % ) =
m
loss
m
0
× 100

During sonication, the effects of AED (25.2, 63.0 and 117.6W/L) and ultrasound duration (5, 10 and 15min) on the subsequent drying kinetics were analyzed using a response surface methodology (RSM). RSM based central composite design was used to generate 13 experimental runs (Table 1 ). Five central points (63.0W/L and 10min) were included in the design. All the experimental trails were carried in a random order and repeated twice. The untreated leaf samples (control samples) and leaf samples soaked in distilled water alone for 15min were used to compare with ultrasound-treated leaf samples in terms of drying kinetics and quality properties.

After removing excess water from the surface of mulberry leaves, the ultrasound-treated samples were placed in a 9-cm glass Petri dish and then transferred to the middle layer of a forced circulating air-drying oven (DHG-9203A, Yiheng Tech Inc., Shanghai, China). The temperature inside the oven was set at 60°C according to the temperature used in the study of Wanyo et al. [3], while the air velocity was maintained at 2.5m/s, which was measured by an anemometer (9871, AZ-Instrument Corp., Taiwan). Furthermore, the air moisture content was determined to be 10%. The air flow was parallel to the Petri dish. Samples were weighted after drying for 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60min in the first 1h. After that, the mass of samples were recorded every 15min until constant weight was reached.

To quantify the effects of different parameters of ultrasound pretreatment on the overall mass transport during subsequent convective drying, a diffusion model based on the Fick’s second law was employed to model the moisture transport in mulberry leaves during drying. Mulberry leaves can be considered as infinite slab geometry since the thickness (approximately 0.2mm) is much smaller than the other two dimensions [15]. Therefore, the governing equation is written as:
(4)
∂ W ( x , t )
∂ t
=
D
e
∂
2
W ( x , t )
∂
x
2

Herein, the effective diffusion coefficient represented the global moisture transport process, including liquid diffusion through solid pores, molecular and vapor diffusion and all the other factors influencing drying characteristics [16]. This model can be solved considering that mulberry leaf was an isotropic and homogenous material, the temperature during drying and initial moisture content were uniform, the effective moisture diffusivity was constant and the shrinkage was negligible.

The initial condition for Eq. (4) is
W ( x , t )
t = 0
=
W
0
, and the boundary conditions are written as:
(5)
∂ W ( x , t )
∂ x
x = 0
= 0
(6)
-
D
e
ρ
DM
∂ W ( x , t )
∂ x
x = L
=
h
m
(
φ
e
-
φ
∞
)

Eq. (5) represented the moisture distribution symmetry while Eq. (6) considered the external mass transfer resistance [16]. In Eq. (6), the water fluxes between mulberry leaf surface and air were equal. Thus, both D e and h m can be quantified after solving the diffusion model. Furthermore, the relative humidity of air at equilibrium was determined according to the sorption isotherms of white mulberry leaves at 60°C reported by Phoungchandang et al. [19].

Considering that Eq. (4) is a parabolic partial differential equation in one space variable x and time t, it was numerically solved by the pdepe function in Matlab, R2010a (The MathWorks, Inc., USA) with the aforementioned initial and boundary conditions [20].Kinetic parameters, including D e and hm , were iteratively adjusted to fit the experimental points, so as to minimize the root mean square error (RMSE, Eq. (7)) between experimental and predicted moisture contents for each drying curve.
(7)
RMSE =

After the identification of D e and hm , the coefficient of determination (R 2, Eq. (8)) and mean relative deviation modulus (E, Eq. (9)) were also calculated to evaluate the goodness of fit.
(8)

2

Furthermore, the dimensionless Sherwood number (Sh) was used to compare the external and internal mass transport resistance, which is calculated as:
(10)
Sh =
h
m
× L
D
e

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of phenolic content and assessment of antioxidant activity
Extraction of phenolic compounds from mulberry leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic compounds from dried mulberry leaves were extracted according to the method described by Katsube et al. [2] with slight modifications. First, dried mulberry leaves were ground into powders. These powders were also used for the measurements of chlorophyll, DNJ and GABA contents in the following sections. Next, 0.2g of mulberry leaf powder was suspended in 20mL of 60% aqueous ethanol solution. Phenolic compounds in mulberry leaves were extracted exhaustively at 40°C in a water bath under agitation for 10h. After centrifugation at 10,000rpm and 4°C for 10min, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.45-μm filter and stored at 4°C until further chemical analysis.

Total phenolic content in mulberry leaves was measured using the Folin–Ciocalteu method [21]. The total phenolic content was standardized against gallic acid and expressed as mg gallic acid equivalents per gram of dried sample.

Total flavonoid content in mulberry leaves was determined by a colorimetric method [3]. Rutin was used to make the calibration curve and the results were expressed as mg Rutin equivalents per gram of dried sample.
Antioxidant activity of dried mulberry leaves was determined by both the 2, 2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline)-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) assay and ferric reducing antioxidant power (FRAP) assay.

The ABTS assay was used to assess the free radical scavenging activity of dried mulberry leaves according to the method of Re et al. [22] with some modifications. ABTS radical cation (ABTṠ+) was produced by reacting 10mL of 7mmol/L ABTS solution with 10mL of 2.45mmol/L potassium persulfate solution in dark at room temperature for 16h. The resulting ABTṠ+ radical solution was diluted properly with 0.2mol/L phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) to obtain absorbance at 734nm of 0.70±0.02. Next, 0.2mL of mulberry leaf extract (with proper dilution) was added to 3.8mL of ABTṠ+ solution. The mixture was mixed thoroughly and allowed to stand at room temperature in dark for 7min. After that, the absorbance at 734nm was recorded and the scavenging capacity was calculated. Trolox was used as the standard and the results were expressed as Trolox equivalent antioxidant capacity (μmol of Trolox per gram of dried sample).

The FRAP assay was performed to evaluate the reducing power of dried mulberry leaves according to the modified version of theBenzie and Strainmethod [23]. Briefly, the FRAP reagent was freshly prepared by mixing 10mmol/L 2,4,6-Tripyridyl-s-Triazine solution in 40mmol/L HCl, 300mmol/L acetate buffer (pH 3.6) and 20mmol/L FeCl3·6H2O at a ratio of 1:10:1. For each assay, 0.1mL of mulberry leaf extract (with proper dilution) was mixed with 0.3mL of deionised water and 3mL of FRAP reagent. The mixture was incubated at 37°C in dark for 8min. The absorbance at 593nm was then measured. FeSO4 solution was used to make the calibration curve. The results were expressed as μmol of FeSO4 equivalents per gram of dried sample.

To measure total chlorophylls content in dried mulberry leaves, 0.2g of aforementioned leaf powder was mixed with 50mL of 95% ethanol solution. Chlorophylls were extracted under agitation in dark for 24h. The extract was filtered and then analyzed by the same UV–Vis spectrophotometer mentioned in Section 2.6.3. The absorbance at 665nm corresponded to chlorophylls a, while the absorbance at 649nm represented chlorophylls b. The contents of chlorophylls a and b were then calculated using the method of Lichtenthaler [24]. Total chlorophylls content was calculated as the sum of contents of chlorophylls a and b and the results were expressed as mg per gram of dried sample.

DNJ content in dried mulberry leaf sample was determined using the method developed by Kim et al. [1]. To be exact, 90mg of mulberry leaf powder was suspended in 2.5mL of 0.05M HCl, vortexed for 15 s and centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 15min. The supernatant was collected and the pellet was extracted again as described above. Both supernatants were pooled and diluted to 6mL using deionized water. Next, the resulting supernatants were derivatized with 9-fluorenylmethyl chloroformate according to the protocols established by Kim et al. [1]. After that, 50μL of derivatized mulberry leaf sample was injected into a HPLC system (Agilent 1200; GMI, Ramsey, MN, USA) connected with a Waters XTERRA MS C18 column (150×4.6mm, 5μm). The column temperature was maintained at 25°C and samples were detected at 254nm using UV–Vis diode-array absorbance detection. The mobile phase was acetonitrile-0.1% aqueous acetic acid (55:45, v/v) and its flowing rate was 1.0mL/min. DNJ concentration was calculated from the calibration curve of DNJ standard and the results were expressed as mg per gram of dried sample.

First, GABA in mulberry leaf powder was extracted and purified following the method described by Bai et al. [25]. Next, the resulting residues were treated according to the method of Syu et al. [26]. Briefly, the residues were dissolvedwith 2mL of NaHCO3 and then centrifuged at 6000rpm for 10min. One mL of supernatant was mixed with 1-mL dabsyl chloride (2mg/mL) in acetone and allowed to react at 67°C for 10min. The reaction was then ended through putting the tubes in an ice bath. Samples were analyzed in the same HPLC system used for DNJ measurement. The column used was an Agilent SB-C18 column (250×4.6mm, 5μm) and the detected wavelength was 425nm. Column temperature was maintained at 30°C. Mobile phase A was acetonitrile while mobile phase B was 0.045M CH3COONa (pH 4). The elution profile was 0–10min, 70%A; 10–12min, 70%A to 62%A; 12–20min, 62%A; 20–22min, 62%A to 55%A; 22–30min, 55%A; 30–36min, 55%A to 40%A; 36–40min, 60%A; 40–41min, 40%A to 30%A; 41–50min, 30%A. Furthermore, the flowing rate was 1mL/min. The results were expressed as mg per gram of dried sample.

Color properties of dried mulberry leaves were measured by a Konica Minolta Chroma Meter (CR-400, Konica Minolta, Japan). This instrument was calibrated with a white standard. Color was recorded using CIE L ∗ a ∗ b ∗ (L ∗: brightness; a ∗: redness-greenness; b ∗: yellowness-blueness) system. Un-fragmented leaves were placed in a 9-cm Petri dish and color analysis was performed in 15 repetitions. For each analysis, samples were arranged in a different way.
Energy consumption during ultrasound pretreatment and convective drying was measured by an energy meter (DDSY879, Shanghai, China).

All the chemical analysis was carried out in triplicate. The results were presented as mean±standard deviation. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed in SPSS 11.5 (SPSS, Inc., USA) to compare the means of quality data about mulberry leaves. Significance was defined at p =0.05 and the Least Significant Difference (Fisher’s LSD) test was used to separate the mean differences. Furthermore, in order to study the effects of AED and ultrasound duration on the weight of mulberry leaves and subsequent drying kinetics, the data about solid loss, weight gain, D e and h m were analyzed using response surface regression and fitted to the following quadraticpolynomial regression model [27]:
(11)
y
i
=
a
0
+
a
1
x
1
+
a
2
x
2
+
a
3
x
1
2
+
a
4
x
2
2
+
a
5
x
1
x
2
the goodness of fit was evaluated by ANOVA in Design-Expert 8.0 (Stat-Ease, Inc., Minneapolis, USA) and response surfaces were then developed using the fitted equations.

Immersion in distilled water resulted in the increase of mulberry leaf weight and ultrasound treatment enhanced the weight gain (Table 1). For example, the weight gain of mulberry leaves soaked in distilled water alone for 15min was 2.49%, whereas the corresponding values of samples treated by ultrasound at 25.2, 63.0 and 117.6W/L for 15min increased to 2.64%, 4.67% and 4.33%, respectively. Similar results were also found in the studies of Kadam et al. [10] Fernandes et al. [11] and Oliveira et al. [12], where ultrasound treatment in distilled water increased the weights of seaweed, melon and apple. The observed changes were mainly due to the incorporation of water in mulberry leaves driven by the concentration gradient of soluble solids between mulberry leaves and distilled water. Meanwhile, it was supposed that the mechanical and cavitation effect of ultrasound could change the cell structure of mulberry leaves, such as generation and broadening of microscopic channels [11]. Consequently, water uptake from transferring medium became easier under ultrasound irradiation.

On the other hand, there was a slight solid loss in mulberry leaves after ultrasound pretreatment (Table 1). The maximum solid loss was observed after sonication at 117.6W/L for 15min. In the study of Romero and Yépez [28], the authors reported that ultrasound pretreatment resulted in the loss of antioxidant compounds in Andean blackberry. Accordingly, the soluble solids lost found in this study probably included components responsible for quality and bioactivity of mulberry leaves, such as phenolics, GABA, DNJ, chlorophyll, etc. Considering that ultrasound-treated samples still needed to be processed by convective drying, it would be more reasonable to analyze the quality of dried mulberry leaves although there was a slight loss of soluble solids after one processing step.

To better understand the effects of AED and ultrasound duration on solid loss and moisture gain of mulberry leaves, the results in Table 1 were processed by Eq. (11). The developed functions are written as:
(12)
Weight gain = 1.063 + 0.069 · AED - 0.200 · t + 6.371 ·
10
- 4
· AED · t - 4.424 ·
10
- 4
·
AED
2
+ 0.014 ·
t
2
(13)
Solid loss = 0.010 + 1.549 ·
10
- 3
· AED - 8.562 ·
10
- 3
· t + 1.681 ·
10
- 4
· AED · t - 8.981 ·
10
- 6
·
AED
2
+ 4.931 ·
10
- 4
·
t
2

According to the ANOVA results, the p-values for both models were statistically significant with values lower than 0.05, while the “lack of fit” p-values for both models were insignificant with values higher than 0.05. Furthermore, the R 2 values for weight gain and solid loss were 0.899 and 0.912, respectively. All these parameters indicated that the resultant quadratic regression equations were reliable to model the variations of weight gain and solid loss of mulberry leaves during ultrasound pretreatment.

Fig. 2 a and b are the combined 3D and contour plots representing the effects of AED and ultrasound duration on weight gain and solid loss of mulberry leaves. As can be seen from Fig. 2a, the amount of water incorporated into mulberry leaves first increased to a peak level and then decreased gradually with the increase of AED. Meanwhile, ultrasound duration had a positive impact on weight gain. However, when ultrasound was used to process seaweeds immersed in distilled water, weight gain decreased with the increase of ultrasound intensity [10]. These phenomena can be explained as low-intensity ultrasound could benefit the formation and broadening of microscopic channels in mulberry leaves [11], whereas high-intensity ultrasound (such as 117.6W/L) could disrupt the structure of mulberry leaves and consequently impede water uptake. On the other hand, although solid loss induced by ultrasound was small, it increased gradually along with the increases of AED and processing time (Fig. 2b). This result was consistent with the studies of Kadam et al. [10] and Oliveira et al. [12], who also found that solid loss in both seaweed and apple during ultrasound pretreatment was positively related with the applied ultrasound intensity and processing time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ultrasound pretreatment on drying kinetics of mulberry leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Ultrasound pretreatment affected the drying kinetics of mulberry leaves positively during convective drying. A representative plot about the drying kinetics of ultrasound-treated and untreated mulberry leaf samples is shown in Fig. 3 . As can be seen, ultrasound treatment before drying enhanced the drying rate, since the moisture content in ultrasound-treated samples decreased faster than that in control samples. Meanwhile, the drying time required to reach a moisture content of 0.10kg water/kg dry matter was determined experimentally. It was found that the drying time was reduced from 101.5min (control sample) to 74.0min when samples were pretreated by ultrasound at 63.0W/L for 10min (central point). Taking the ultrasound processing time into consideration, the aforementioned ultrasound treatment reduced the total processing time (ultrasound treatment time+convective drying time) by 17.2%. Analogous results were also found when ultrasound processing was conducted prior to dehydration of seaweed, parsley and apple, the drying time of which can be shortened by up to 12.6%, 29.8% and 40.0%, respectively [10,13,29]. Besides, immersion in distilled water did not affect the drying kinetics of mulberry leaves positively, which was not included in Fig. 3.

Regarding the effects of AED and ultrasound duration on the drying time of mulberry leaves, the drying time generally decreased with the increase of AED and ultrasound duration. The drying time reached the minimum (approximately 71.0min) when samples were sonicated at 117.6W/L for 15min, while the drying time for samples pretreated at 25.2W/L for 5min was close to that of control samples, being 101.0min. Both the studies of Kadam et al. [10] and Romero and Yépez [28] also reported that drying time of seaweed and blackberry decreased with the increase of amount of input ultrasound energy.

Besides, the energy consumptions for all ultrasound pretreatments and convective drying processes were measured. For control samples and samples soaked in distilled water alone for 15min, the energies consumed to decrease the moisture content to 0.10kg water/kg dry matter were 0.81 and 0.90 kw h, respectively. On the other hand, when mulberry leaves were pretreated by ultrasound at 63.0W/L for 10min, the total energy consumed was 0.67 kw h (0.08 kw h for ultrasound pretreatment and 0.59kWh for drying). Compared to control samples, the total energy consumption was saved by 17.3%. Moreover, the total energy consumed to process mulberry leaves sonicated at 117.0W/L for 15min was 0.68 kw h (0.12 kw h for ultrasound pretreatment and 0.56kwh for drying). As can be seen, ultrasound application at proper conditions before convective drying can lead to the decrease of total energy consumption. Similar results were also reported by Sledz et al. [13], when ultrasound was used to process parsley leaves before drying. Due to the reduction of energy consumption, the cost of manufacturing mulberry leaf tea can also be reduced by ultrasound pretreatment.

To deeply clarify the influence of ultrasound pretreatment on the mass transfer phenomena during subsequent drying, the drying kinetics was modeled by the diffusion model considering external resistance to mass transfer. In this case, the effect of ultrasound pretreatment of both internal and external resistance to moisture transfer was studied through quantifying D e and h m, respectively [15]. Generally, the results provided by the diffusion model under all the processing conditions were satisfactory, due to high values of R 2, as well as relatively low values of RMSE and E (Table 2 ). Moreover, the simulated lines for control samples and samples sonicated at 63.0W/L for 10min are illustrated in Fig. 3. As can be seen, the experimental and predicted data about the moisture content in mulberry leaves followed similar trends.

In the current experimental range, D e values varied from 1.66×10−10 to 3.01×10−10 m2/s (Table 2), which were in the similar range of D e obtained from mulberry leaf drying by tray and heat pump dehumidified dryers [19]. Both D e and h m values of ultrasound-treated samples were higher than those of samples without ultrasound pretreatment, indicating that internal and external moisture transports during drying were improved. For example, D e and h m values of control samples were 1.66×10−10 m2/s and 0.89×10−4 kgwater/m2 s, while the corresponding values of samples pretreated by ultrasound at 63.0W/L for 10min increased to (2.55±0.05)×10−10 m2/s and (1.19±0.01)×10−4 kgwater/m2 s. Similar results were also found in the study of Romero and Yépez [28], who reporting that D e of Andean blackberry during convective drying at 60°C increased from 2.91×10−10 m2/s to 4.32×10−10 m2/s when samples were pretreated by ultrasound. As mentioned previously, ultrasound pretreatment could create microscopic channels or even disrupt the cell structure of mulberry leaves, making the moisture removal inside easier [11]. Meanwhile, the thickness of boundary layer could be reduced during sonication, resulting in the decrease of external resistance to moisture transfer [16]. Furthermore, it should be pointed out that immersion in distilled water alone for 15min also slightly increased the moisture effective diffusivity (1.70×10−10 m2/s). This result was probably due to the swelling of inner structure of mulberry leaves, which needs to be clarified in future studies.

The data about D e and h m presented in Table 2 were processed by Eq. (11) and the resulting equations are written as:
(14)

2

These data were fitted well to Eqs. (14) and (15), since both models had high values of R 2 (R 2 >0.95), significant p-values and insignificant “lack of fit” p-values. The effects of AED and ultrasound duration on D e and h m are visualized in Fig. 2c and d. As can be seen, the increase of AED and ultrasound duration during ultrasound pretreatment increased both D e and h m values. However, the magnitudes of enhancements in D e and h m at high AED levels were small. In the current experiment range, mulberry leaf samples pretreated by ultrasound at 117.6W/L for 15min had the highest values of D e and h m, which were 0.68 and 0.40 times higher than that of samples sonicated at 25.2W/L for 5min.

Furthermore, the dimensionless Sherwood number was calculated for each drying treatment and the results are shown in Table 2. It can be seen that the Sherwood number of ultrasound-treated samples was lower than that of samples without ultrasound treatment, implying that the magnitude of enhancement in h m induced by ultrasound pretreatment was lower than that of enhancement in D e. Therefore, ultrasound pretreatment before drying had greater influence on internal mass transfer resistance than on external mass transfer resistance [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quality assessment of dried mulberry leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Next, several physical and chemical properties of dried mulberry leaves with and without ultrasound pretreatment were analyzed. The samples sonicated at 63.0W/L for 10min (central point) were selected as the representative one to compare with control samples. The results are summarized in Table 3 .

To start with, color is an important property of food products. The values of L ∗ and b ∗ for ultrasound-pretreated mulberry leaves were slightly higher than that of control samples. Sledz et al. [13] also reported that ultrasound pretreatment could slightly increase the L ∗ and b ∗ values of dried parsley leaves in certain conditions. However, the effect of ultrasound pretreatment on color properties of dried parsley leaves depended on the processing parameters selected during drying. Meanwhile, there was no significant (p ⩾0.05) difference in a ∗ value between ultrasound-pretreated samples and control samples, indicating the same greenness. Considering that chlorophylls are the major pigments in mulberry leaves, the total chlorophylls contents of dried mulberry leaf samples were determined. Interestingly, the total chlorophylls content in ultrasound-treated samples was 0.21mg/g higher than that of control samples. It was probably due to the shorter drying time required for ultrasound-treated samples. Moreover, Sabater and Rodrigues reported that water floating can provide a protective effect against chlorophyll degradation in detached barley leaves through decreasing the chlorophyllase activity, particularly at high temperatures [31]. Therefore, the potential protective effect of water may also contribute to the higher chlorophyll content in mulberry leaves soaked in distilled water with and without ultrasound irradiation than control samples. The relationship between chlorophyll content and color properties of dried mulberry leaves needs to be clarified in future studies.

Ultrasound-treated samples had the highest total phenolic content (65.83±0.88mg/g), followed by control samples (64.08±1.13mg/g) and samples immersed in distilled water alone (62.67±0.38mg/g). At the same time, the total flavonoid content in ultrasound-treated samples (64.32±1.69mg/g) was comparable to that of control samples (64.29±1.66mg/g), while the total flavonoid content in samples immersed in distilled water alone (59.74±2.46mg/g) was slightly lower than that of other samples. On the other hand, since phenolic compounds are the main contributor to mulberry leaf antioxidant ability [2], the antioxidant activity of dried samples were measured by both ABTS radical scavenging ability and FRAP assay. As can be seen in Table 3, the ABTS radical scavenging ability of ultrasound-treated samples was slightly higher than that of samples without ultrasound pretreatment, although the differences in these samples were statistically insignificant (p ⩾0.05). Furthermore, the FRAP value of ultrasound-treated samples was significantly (p< 0.05) higher than that of other samples, being 286.12±6.46μmol FeSO4/g dried sample. Romero and Yépez [28] found that ultrasound pretreatment before drying resulted in the decrease of antioxidant activity of Andean blackberry. However, the aforementioned results revealed that both phenolic content and antioxidant activity of final mulberry leaf products pretreated by ultrasound were comparable to, or even slightly higher than that of control samples, although ultrasound pretreatment also led to a solid loss in mulberry leaves. This result can be explained by the reason that the reduction in drying time after ultrasound pretreatment could alleviate the damage to phenolics and nutritional value of mulberry leaves caused by thermal drying, thus offsetting the loss in phenolic compounds and antioxidant activity after sonication. In most cases, shorter drying time and lower drying temperature (⩽60°C) can preserve antioxidant capacity and phenolic compounds in mulberry leaves, whereas drying at temperatures higher than 70°C can result in significant decreases of phenolic compounds and antioxidant activity [2,3].

Besides phenolic compounds, DNJ and GABA are another two important bioactive compounds in mulberry leaves. The former one is a potent α-glucosidase inhibitor, while the latter one has the antihypertensive ability [1,4]. DNJ content in control samples was 3.11mg/g, which was close to the corresponding content in the shoots of several Thai mulberry varieties, but higher than the content in these young Thai mulberry leaves [32]. Meanwhile, GABA content in control samples was 0.72mg/g, which stayed in the similar range of GABA content in the young leaf of a Korean mulberry variety named Subong [33]. According to Table 3, there were no significant differences (p ⩾0.05) in DNJ and GABA contents among samples with and without ultrasound pretreatment.

Overall, ultrasound pretreatment had no negative impact on quality properties and nutritional values of dried mulberry leaves. The bioactive compounds in mulberry leaves pretreated by ultrasound were retained well after drying.

Ultrasound has been demonstrated as an effective pretreatment to enhance the convective drying process of mulberry leaves. For example, the total processing time and energy consumption needed to reduce moisture content in mulberry leaves to 0.10kg water/kg dry matter was decreased by 17.2% and 17.3%, when samples were pretreated by ultrasound at 63.0W/L for 10min (central point). The modeling results provided by the diffusion model considering external transfer resistance revealed that the increase of AED and ultrasound duration could increase D e and h m in the current experimental range. Meanwhile, ultrasound pretreatment had a deeper influence on internal moisture transport than on external moisture transfer during drying.

On the other hand, although there was a slight solid loss in mulberry leaves after ultrasound pretreatment, the quality properties, such as color, antioxidant activity and contents of bioactive compounds of dried mulberry leaves pretreated by ultrasound (63.0W/L, 10min) were comparable to those of control samples. In conclusion, ultrasound pretreatment in a proper way can shorten the drying time of mulberry leaves and save the energy consumed, while the quality of final product can also be satisfactory. Future studies should pay attention to scale-up of ultrasound equipment based on the identified D e and h m.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Independent Innovation of Agricultural Sciences in Jiangsu Province, China coded CX(15)1026.
